items.title: Is this sun/heat damage?
items.body: Spotted Dead Nettle is supposed to be drought tolerant but I'm considering eradicating it from this mulch bed in favor of something else. It gets leggy like this every year. Is this from too much sun? I tried watering it every day and this still occurred.

items.title: Yucca elephantipes leaves drying
items.body: My yucca’s leaves are browning and drying out in the middle. I have had it for 5 years. I repotted this spring. In summer I water every 1-2 weeks when soil is dry and about 1x month in winter. It gets south facing light and I rotate it every few months. Could rotating it be the problem? This is my favorite plant, help!!

items.title: What is the most likely diagnosis for this physialis leaf/plant?
items.body:
The plant seems healty overall, but it seems focused on growing fruits and is not producing a lot of new growth compared to it's companions. The plant lives in a 12(?)L pot and has been protected from rainfall. New leafs seem fine, but one might imagine they also have a very thin darker edge, but no spots elsewhere. Other plants nearby have similar changes, but to a varying degree, and some with very sparse changes of this kind. Indoor plants are totaly free from this (reduces odds of fertilization issue IMO)

Some suggestions are overfertilization (since I thought it was smart to feed the fruiting plant a bit more than those not fruiting but compared to tomtao plants I've fed them sparsly), some fungal infection or perhaps just temperature stress related to cold nights (slightly above 10C) but I really don't know. I could not find pictures online that looked very similar (tried for tomatoes as well as they are related and I'm pretty sure I saw something similar on tomatoes last year). I added a partly in focus pic through a magnifier. The changes seem primarily to be on the top side of the leaf.

Thx for any suggestions.

items.title: Why is my pineapple not growing bigger?
items.body:
I planted my pineapple about 5 years ago from the head of a store bought pineapple and even though it hasn't died and is still growing, it hasn't got nearly big enough to produce fruit or even approached the size I've seen other planted pineapples get.

About a year ago I planted it in a new pot with fresh soil and compost, it seemed to get a little better. I noticed what looked like smallish pineapples growing near the bottom. But beside that it's mostly still the same.

I water it as often as I can remember (usually every second or so day), and keep it in full sun. Is there anything I should be doing that I'm not?

items.title: Pink Flower Identification
items.body: Do you recognize this spring blooming pink flower with delicate foliage planted in the fall as a small corm? Tuber?

items.title: Early blight is affecting my tomatoes - how long after lesions appear should I scrap the whole plant instead of trimming leaves?
items.body: I've read that when a tomato plant is infected with early blight you should trim the leaves with spots on them, but am wondering at what point the trimming isn't worth it anymore. Is there an amount of time after I see spots appear that I should give up on the plant fully?

items.title: Can Aeschynomene aspera be used in constructed wetland for filtering grey water?
items.body:
Updated the question to be more specific, after the first answer.

Can Aeschynomene aspera be used in constructed wetland for filtering grey water in Tropical Climate, especially in Eastern India (West Bengal)?

Aeschynomene aspera grows in watery area but I have not seen their mention in grey water filtration, possibly due to the the sparse documentation regarding this topic based on West Bengal, India geography.

items.title: At what age is it optimal to repot plants?
items.body:
This spring I seeded radish, pepper and tomatoes. As I feel it a waste to just thin them out, after they've grown their first pair of true leaves(i.e. not cotyledon) I repot them more sparsely. I use store-bought soil+compost mix. I water before repotting to help the soil hold together and after planting to help the roots bind to the soil. I attempt to not damage the roots ... but apparently incompetently as most have withered. The peppers in a weird way - from the top down. I attempt to allow them get as mature as possible ... but then the roots twist together, making the procedure even more damaging.

What are some tips for successful repottig of seedling? Keeping the soil far too damp is a possible cause but what others can there be? What amount of sunlight are they supposed to receive in the initial couple of weeks?

items.title: Does my rose flower have another flower bud in it?
items.body:
Title says it all... what's going on with my rose flower?

[Update]: The inside flower has now also opened!

items.title: Why does my bougainvillea flower but not generate leaves?
items.body:
I have had this bougainvillea for more than a year. It gets several hours of direct sunlight, and every once in a while it flowers .. but other than shortly after I got it, it hasn't grown new branches or many leaves. When it flowers it looks like the attached picture, but eventually the flowers fall and it remains with relatively few flowers/leaves until this happens again. On the other hand, it hasn't died this whole time and the flowering cycle has happened at least three times.

What am I doing wrong or what should I do differently?

items.title: “Why are my basil leaves transparent?”
items.body:
Run

How do I cure my basil of such disease? I tried spraying it with baking soda with soap and cooking oil. Thanks.

items.title: Small pond with frogs is covered with duckweed. Is this OK?
items.body:
My fiancée and I bought our first house last December and the previous owners did a fantastic job with the garden. Beautiful plants, shrubs etc, and a small pond (estimate about 2m x 1m and 2m deep). Up until a few weeks ago the water was fairly clear with a few lilies. I noticed we had frogs (I counted 8) and they produced frog spawn which has since given birth to lots of tadpoles, although I haven't seen them.

The problem now is that we've suddenly hit summer and duckweed has blanketed the pond. I would like to manage the duckweed as I know it's hard to get rid of completely, but I'm not sure if I'll be harming the tadpoles and frogs if I remove it as they might like it?

I have absolutely no idea, its the first time I've had a garden with grass let alone an ecosystem! So any advice would be really appreciated.

items.title: flower suggestion for outside the window
items.body:
first of all I'm not a gardening expert so I don't know the correct terms.

I've recently put a pot outside my window and I've put some roses chinensis in it, but all its flowers fell off due to winds and also some of them dried out (the seller suggested me these flowers).

Now I need a flower for there, and I need it to be acid rain resistant (the air is polluted in here so sometimes we've got acid rains :D ), wind resistant, and highly resistant to high temperatures (42 degrees for example), and also sun hungry (here it's sunny most the time :D ). the humidity is also not too high here (for example right now it's 12%).

thanks a lot.

What fencing specifications (mesh size, height, material, and installation method) are recommended for this purpose?

items.body:
I've ordered a bunch of raspberry plants from a reputable nursery here in Italy. One of them as this black streak that start from the base and goes upwards. It also withers the leaves.

I don't think this is normal, maybe it's some kind of disease, but I can't pinpoint it.

I peeled the bark a bit, as you can see in the photos, and it's a bit brownish underneath.

Thanks in advance.

Thanks for your time.

items.title: Is the Worx leaf mulcher the easiest way to chop up a LOT of oak leaves?
items.body:
I have about 1.3 acres with a lot of oak trees. AND a tick problem, who thrive in moist environments like under leaves. So in my war against them last year I started raking, bagging, and bringing the leaves to the dump. Two trips with a rented trailer only made a dent. (I used to just leave them on the ground cover and under bushes etc., so granted it will be easier as time goes on.)

I have them piled up to eventually compost, but I decided I need to speed the process by chopping them. We tried mowing over them but it is time consuming and [not great for the mower.](I've been told that isn't true).

Now I am looking into buying a leaf mulcher. This Worx model was the highest rated on Bob Vila and also, for a stand-alone, on Popular Mechanics. I already have a leaf blower, and I think a vacuum thingamajiggy will just slow me down. Am I wrong? (I guesstimate I have between 5-10,000 gallons of leaves. Maybe more.)

Or should I buy a special mulching lawn mower? Or hire someone with a leaf mulcher as mentioned by Ecnerwal in the comments?

Does anyone have experience with the linked mulcher? I've read reviews, they are kind of all over the place for all the solutions I've investigated, and you also have to wonder how valid they are in the first place.

Thank you so much for any wisdom offered!

[p.s. It has definitely helped reduce the ticks in my yard!]

items.title: Would Diatomaceous Earth work for spraying unreachable Tent Caterpillar nests?
items.body:
In trying to help answer another person's question, I realized that I have one of my own.

I try to manually remove the tent moths from my trees every year, using both a step ladder and extension ladder. But, I still can't reach them all. I live in Virginia, USA.

How effective/harmful would spraying Diatomateous Earth in liquid form be (using a hose sprayer) be on those tents that I can't reach, so that they don't spread? We would only be specifically spraying the tents. (And no, I don't use it in my gardens or yard, myself.)

I have a very sweet neighbor who has different views on things. (He loves grass. Not gardens, bugs, and birds.) We have always been able to compromise, and he no longer sprays pesticides and weed killers onto my property with his hose, but we do have some trees that border our properties, and I am hoping to come up with a solution that will make us both happy this year.

I welcome any and all advice (including alternative solutions) as an answer to this question! Thanks.

(Added info) The hose jet-streaming method (although being the ideal solution) has not worked to break open these tents unless you are pretty close to them. It doesn't work on the higher tents, even when I am trying to shoot them from a ladder, with the strongest water pressure that we have.

items.title: Yellow leaves with green spots on apple tree
items.body:
I've had apple trees for years, but this is the firt year I have really started paying any attention to the orchard. I am located in eastern Europe, we've had a really cold winter this year (temperatures as low as -30°C) so many of my trees have suffered cold damage. The tree in question was damaged too, as a result I had to remove one branch when the effects of the frost became apparent (very little leaves appeared on it and then they wilted and died). Prior to that the tree was pruned in early spring and sprayed with bordeaux mixture against fungal diseases. Recently I've noticed that there are patches on the trunk where the phloem layer of the bark is either discoloured or dead, although it's hard to assess the severity of that.

When the leaves really started coming in on the tree in question i noticed that on some branches the leaves have a distinct yellowish tint, meanwhile in other places the leaves don't exibit any discoloration:

Searching the web yielded chlorosis due to the lack of iron as a possible cause. I've watered the tree with iron(II) citrate solution, but the problem persists. Another worrying symptom is the fact that little to none new growth is happening on the branches with discolored leaves.

Last week we've had quite a bit of rain and I've noticed a new pattern emerge: small yellow leaves with green spots, located normally at the base of leaf rosettes: These leaves separate from the branches without any noticeable force. It's hard to tell if new ones are appearing, but I've been doing my best removing them.

Some leaves also take on a more off-green, swampish kind of color, later they seem to die completely. Both the spotted and the brown leaves occur more or less all around the tree, even on branches where all the other leaves are lush green.

I recognize that there's probably more than one problem with my tree but I would really like to know what at least some of those could be and what can I do to combat them and nurse this tree back to health.

items.title: What to do with my baby pineapple: Parenting 101
items.body:
I'm having a baby pineapple plant - it has been growing pretty well.

At this point the pineapple seems to become too heavy, the plant is leaning towards a side and the stick on which it is attached feels a bit softer than usual. Not in an extreme matter though.

I'm unsure what to do with it? Is the pineapple ripe for harvesting and should I just cut it off? How do I see that he is ready? Should I then use the top of the baby to get a new plant?

What to do with the old plant? Remove the stick? There are also smaller baby plants (without a stick) growing on the side of the mother plant. Should I just pull these out and put them in a separate pot?

Thanks in advance.

I attach another image to get a clearer view of the pineapple itself:

items.title: To be effective, how long does Glyphosate weed killer need to be applied before it rains?

This morning I awoke to the sound of rain. Will the rain have diluted the glyphosate or will my weeds die?

To make this more generic:

To be 100% effective how long before rain should I poison?

I also have fungus gnats, coming from my soil, would you think it can be the larvae? I read they are not causing anything to the plant and rarely to the roots.. well ?

  1. what is it?

  2. an idea to treat them? (Organically if possible). Please note I have soil blocks, which then render complicated some treatments

I tried to splint the stem with a pencil and tape to hold it up. Is this a worthless effort? Should I cut the stems off?

How fast can I expect the stems to grow back? This is planted in a 16 gallon pot on a south facing balcony. What are some tips to make the recovery fast?

items.title: What happens if I use fishing line in a grass trimmer?
items.body:
Grass trimmer cutting line looks pretty much like very thick fishing line. So it looks like if I find fishing line of exactly the same diameter that is accepted by my grass trimmer head it should be okay. Meanwhile the grass trimmer manufacturers only recommend specially crafted cutting line.

Is there any difference? What happens if I use fishing line in a grass trimmer?

items.title: Cabbage, Eggplant, and Cauliflower with white-ish dry spots
items.body: I've got eggplant, cabbage and cauliflower seedlings growing inside in peat moss with some all-around fertilizer (24-8-6). However, the edges of their leaves are drying and curling up, and some of them have dry, beige spots in the middle. I thought it might have been a calcium deficiency, so I boiled some eggshells and added them to each of them. I've attached an image of the cabbage seedlings. It's been 2 days now, and their condition is deteriorating. First of all, is this indeed a calcium deficiency? And if so, what should I do to rapidly restore the leaves?

items.title: Can sunflower be used in Three Sisters planting instead of corn?
items.body:
The Native American Three Sisters technique traditionally uses corn, beans, and squash as companion plants. The corn provides a trellis for the beans, the beans fix nitrogen for the corn, and the squash creates a cover layer on the ground to retain moisture (kinda like a living mulch).

I'm not that interested in growing corn, so instead I'm considering sunflower. I'm not sure, however, if corn traditionally interacts with the soil nutrients in the companion relationship in some important way that sunflowers wouldn't replicate. Could sunflowers make a good companion plant in a Three Sisters plot?

items.title: Lowering Soil pH with Diluted Sulphuric Acid
items.body:
I want to lower my soil pH from 7 to 4.5 with dilute sulphuric acid. It's time to buy potted blueberry plants here in El Dorado County, CA (Zone 9A) so I would like to adjust the pH quickly rather than waiting for elemental sulphur to convert to sulphuric acid in the soil over several months.

My soil is a 50-50 mix of clay soil and rich compost with perlite added, and no commercial potting soil. I will be growing my blueberries in 20 in dia containers shaped like half barrels.

I would appreciate a recommendation on the level of dilution for 93% sulphuric acid (I'm thinking 3 tablespoons of acid to a gallon of distilled water) and the application rate. Should the application rate be based on the surface area or the volume of soil in the container? After obtaining the desired pH, perhaps after several applications as determined by monitor pH, how long should I wait before adding 2 potted blueberry plants to the container?

items.title: Why is my Physialis peruviana (Cape gooseberry) dropping flower (-body) soon after pollination?
items.body:
My Physialis peruviana are all dropping their fruiting bodies. I have pollinated the flowers by q-tip/shaking and they seem to develop a very tiny fruit but the fruit-stem gets yellow/pale before the entire thing drops off a week or so thereafter.

They were sown late february, and have been growing nicely. Plants are kept in 15L-5L pots and all look healthy. Some are now outside(10C-25C) and some are kept inside but this doesn't seem to make a difference so far. There have been no visible pests or diseases. The plants in the largest pots have grown huge (2-3 metres tall) and have failed to flower and instead drop the flower buds so they are probably still juveniles.

My fear is too long photoperiod, so I have restricted it for some of the indoor plants, but it has not resolved it so far. I have also tried very sparse vs moderate tomato fertilizer with no difference and watering seems to be on point by the looks of the plants. I always have had great sucess growing tomatoes & chilli peppers under similar conditions.

Do anyone have real life experience with growing this plant (preferably in northern parts of the world)? What is the likely cause of dropping developing fruit soon after pollination? And why are some dropping their flower bud before even flowering?

UPDATE june 20th: The two plants producing flowers(now outside) are on track for producing fruit, suggesting pollination might have been the issue. However, the rest of the plants(inside or outside) have not started flowering. Photoperiod/ initial artificial light usage is still my main suspect for these. I didn't perform the light restriction experiment properly.

items.title: If I just leave dandelions and clover in my lawn, what problems may happen?
items.body:
There is a lot of advice on the internet about getting rid of dandelions and clover from a lawn: chemical herbicides, manual digging, organic weed remedies. I live in Denver, Colorado, USDA zone 5. I'm curious about the end-state of my strategy:

  1. The lawn is mature and has a mix of grasses, but mostly it's kentucky blue grass.
  2. I don't use any herbicides on the lawn.
  3. I don't try to manually remove the dandelions or clover by digging them out.
  4. I mow the lawn once a week at about 3 inches, the tallest setting on my mower, and mulch the cut grass back onto the lawn (I don't bag it).
  5. I aerate the soil and top-dress with a well-screened compost once a year.
  6. I water using a Rachio, which I love.

I've been managing this way for about 2 years now and am curious what the end state will be?

Aside from getting the periodic side-glance from neighbors who prefer a world without dandelions, what will happen? Will the lawn remain a mix of grasses, clover and dandelions or will one weed become more dominant? Are there any weeds that are so bad that if they appear I should work hard to remove them?

items.title: Do dandelions help or inhibit the growth of white clover?
items.body:
I am trying to get white Dutch clover to take over my yard, where there is some kind of grass(es) already there. The clover is spreading nicely in many spots.

There’s also plenty of dandelions growing.

I’ve been picking the dandelions, removing them with a couple inches of their roots, but now I’m reading about the benefits of dandelions for the soil.

So now I’m thinking about just letting the dandelions grow and grow.

What I’m wondering is … will the dandelions inhibit the growth of the clover, help promote and nurture clover growth, or not necessarily either one?

Note: I’m not asking whether or not dandelions or clover are good for soil health; my understanding is that they are… I’m asking whether or not I should be letting dandelions grow, when my main goal is to have clover take over the yard.

And if I do let the dandelions grow (instead of pulling them up by the roots), is it good (for the adjacent clover) to let the dandelion flowers grow and go to seed first, pick the flowers before they go to the white puffball seed, just mow the dandelions with or without their flowers or seed whenever I mow the clover, or what?

Again, I’m asking these questions for the benefit of the clover.

2nd priority is the grass. If the grass dies off because of the clover, that’s fine, but I don’t want a lawn of dandelions at the cost of minimizing the grass or the clover - UNLESS it is a temporary means to an end, wherein the dandelions are aerating the soil and bringing minerals and nutrients up to the top, that will lead to a proliferation of clover and/or grass, in the not too distant future.

Thanks!

items.title: Why is my plant losing so many leaves?
items.body:
This is my Philodendron (specifically the Philodendron Burle Marx variety), I bought it 3 months ago.

When I bought it, the leaves were large, dark green, and very dense. Now it has started shedding leaves one after other, and the colour of the leaves have also turned light greenish yellowish.

In fact, I was told that it an indoor plant, but all the leaves started drooping. Honestly the plant's condition worsens when I keep it indoors, so I started keeping it in my balcony.

I am not sure what to do, as I am very new to gardening.

(I don't know if it helps, but I live in Hyderabad, India and here it is really hot and dry with temperatures reaching around 40 degrees in Summer (which usually lasts from mid April to the first week of June))

Edit : Unfortunately my plant died, as its roots had rotted. When I was trying to put another plant, I realised there was a snake 😱 (it was shiny black, that was the only detail I could see) .Could that be one of the reason ?

items.title: What's wrong with my jasmin?
items.body: I have several Arabian Jasmine plants, some for years. Most are on a third-floor balcony,but recently I also planted some in the yard. One of the jasmines in the yard, and one of the plants in the balcony have been looking worse lately - almost like they're drying up, though I think I've been watering them well. I also think I see something on the leaves, though I'm not 100% sure. Can anyone identify what is wrong with them?

items.title: How can I tell if my tomato plant is stressed or supposed to have tiny tomatoes?
items.body:
I got some tomato seeds, and I was pretty sure they were regular tomatoes, but now that a few are ready for harvest they are very small. Now I'm not sure if I actually got a cherry tomato plant or if the plant is stressed and growing very small tomatoes because of that. It may be that the plant is not getting enough direct sunlight, but I can't say for sure.

Will a stressed plant still be able to provide ripe although small fruit? Can you tell from the images if this is a cherry tomato or regular? Does the plant look stressed?

items.title: Is this Thuja Green Giant overwatered or underwatered?
items.body:
I planted 5 1ft Thuja Green Giants from fastgrowingtrees.com in the winter and they all died from overwatering. At first I followed the watering guide that the website recommends but then noticed one was dying so I got worried and ended up flooding all of them.

I'm now on my second batch of green giants (planted in the spring) and the summer has reached triple digit temperatures. (I'm in southern Idaho). I've been watering all my green giants via sprinkler every other day, expect for the tree shown in the photo because the sprinklers don't reach it. To compensate, I've been lightly watering it every other day with the hose. This is the spot where the first one died last time, and it's the first to start dying this time. The needles just turned brittle. I watered yesterday and tested the soil moisture this morning and found it's on the low end of moist. I'm hesitant to water it more for fear of overwatering (the website recommends twice a week in hot temperatures at this stage, and I've already doubled that).

Can anyone tell by the photo whether it needs more or less water? It's planted in MiracleGro Tree & Shrub soil with mulch on top.

Thanks!

items.title: What is this possibly edible plant?
items.body: What is this plant? I planted Pak choy seeds in my cold room (15° C) and by accident this fellow grew out of the soil. It's leaves are thick (about 1 mm). The growth pattern is symmetrical. Google told me that this might be Brazilian spinach but so far I am not convinced.

items.title: Silver Tree (Leucadendron Argentum) seedling Brown/Crispy Leaves
items.body:
I have an approximately 8-month old silver tree (Leucadendron Argentum) seedling that I grew from seeds I got in South Africa. I planed them in a very well-draining soil composed of sand, pelite, and peat and watered it every 5 days or so. It has been in a spot with direct sun for at least 3-4 hours/day and protected from high-winds whenever possible. Outside temps have dropped to the 40s so I have brought it inside to a bright south-facing window.

It has been relatively healthy until the last month where some of the newer leaves (but not the older ones) have been turning brownish/gray and crispy. I have increased watering frequency to every ~3 days, but not sure if its helping.

Any advice on the problem based on the description (wasnt able to add images for some reason)?

Its a beautiful plant and I'd hate to lose it.

items.title: Disease on Morning Glories
items.body: Can you tell me what has killed my beautiful morning glories this year? I am just sick as they covered the whole side of my house. How do I keep this from happening again?😢

items.title: How do I find out & treat what is eating my hibiscus leaves
items.body:
I was wondering what might be eating away at my hibiscus and how do I protect my poor little plant.

I don't visually see any bugs on or under the leaves.

Update: This plant is in a pot. Location: North Texas.

items.title: How to save this peace lily?
items.body:
I need help saving my peace lily! We bought this plant around 2024, and it has now started looking very unhealthy.

The leaves are drooping, curling, turning yellow/brown, and drying at the edges. The base of the plant looks dark and wet. Some leaves still have a little green, so I’m hoping it can be saved.

I’m not sure if this is because of overwatering, root rot, poor drainage, lack of sunlight, fungal growth, or something else. The plant is kept indoors. I however highly think it might be due to oversunlight and high temperature, I live in a state where temperatures can go to 45 celsius especially during the summer and lets just say we are not the best gardeners, I sometimes even forget to water, also it was left outside since the past couple of years, since it was performing really well, but now I also shifted to the much hotter city that I talked about the 45 Celsius one, looking at it being unhealthy I decided to shift it inside but I hope it can be saved rn. A key detail I noticed are the green leaves are unusually crispy

What should I do now?

P.S: Any plant-keeping and watering tips as well would be very appreciated!

P.S: As people suggested I will try to remove it from shade, and put it in my AC room so the temperature stays cool, I will also cut leaf

P.S: When it was in sunlight, a shaded one albeit, few new leafs started growing, they are kind of crispy, but should I still keep in shade?

items.title: Why are all my indoor plants looking shabby even my seedlings are dying
items.body: So the bug matched what a thrip looks like. And the tops of their soil looks like it's just not doing so well. It's not dark brown or black in color it's like faded. Or has a skim over it. Kinda hard to describe. Plus one of my philodendron and my wandering jew has been dug into. Like half the root systems are showing. I cannot imagine what done that? Help. I haven't done anything yet except went and bought new potting mix and a bottle of captain jacks dead bugs. Have not used it tho.

items.title: Small white bugs clustered by pea plant
items.body: Small white bugs clustered by pea plant- seem to be killing it. What is it and how can I be rid of it safely?

items.title: Will cat poo affect my flowers?
items.body:
We have two cats at home, one of them uses the indoor litter tray while the other prefers to use the flower bed (then diligently spreading soil to cover up the deed).

Should I be concerned? The flower bed in question is seeded with lilies and sunflowers.

items.title: I used Epsom salts to water cucumber plants and the next day they are dying
items.body: Can anyone please let me know if I can bring them back? I used Epsom salts. They were doing great and now it looks like they are all going to die

items.title: What can be done with an aloe vera leaf that fell off and still looks healthy? Start a new plant?
items.body:
I had asked this question (with no replies) about a healthy looking leaf suddenly falling off an aloe vera.

I'm wondering what I could do with the leaf? It is still healthy looking and plump. I would like to start a new aloe plant in a different pot, but from what I red this is often unsuccessful when using a leaf. Is there anything else I can try? Anyway of preserving an aloe leaf for later use, for example I've heard of making ice cubes to apply to sun burns (but it's almost winter now).

From the look of the leaf, is there a fungus on the plant? I just re potted it with fresh dirt :(

[3 I had put the fallen off leaf into a bag. I notice the liquid that evaporated into the bag is more of a brown color than like water.

items.title: What are these red cocoons in my trees leaves?
items.body: The tree In my garden as lots of red cocoons on its leaves, I assume these are insects, do I need to be concerned or are they harmless?

items.title: New lawn not rolled, just mud
items.body: The contractor we used did not level the 4 to 6 inches of topsoil after seating. Because our property is sloped the grass is now almost 6 inches high, but we cannot get on it to mow it because you just sink in the mud. What do we do? We are in zone five, I believe, so frost will be coming soon. Do we wait until spring? What do we do with this long grass that we cannot get cut?

items.title: I applied nemetodes 2 weeks ago. Now each morning I find many leather jackets on the path and patio
items.body: I applied nematodes to my lawn 2 weeks ago. Now each morning I find many leather jackets on the path and patio. Is this a sign the treatment is working, or the infestation getting worse?

items.title: Insect control and bioadvance
items.body:
I am mixing 3-1 Rose and Plant Systemic Food and Control with water instead of attaching to watering hose.

The Product Label says "Apply at a rate of 3 fl oz per 10 sq. ft", but it does not say how concentrated the solution should be for application.

How much of it do I use when mixing with water instead of attaching to watering hose?

items.title: Can't get cucumbers past seed leaves
items.body:
Last year I tried to grow some Boston Pickling cucumbers. They grew to about 2 inches and just as they got their first true leaves they wilted and died. I had just moved house and had planted those indoors on a heated propagator in mid May (I used that method to grow Vorgebirgstrauben once at my last house and it was a success). I chalked this up to bad luck.

Now this year I've tried to grow Vorgebirgstrauben and also National Pickling varieties and they have followed the exact same pattern. I propagated in mid April, seed leaves grew and then the plant just wilts.

So now I've started a second run of National Pickling, this time not on the propagator but in a greenhouse at the start of May. 2 weeks later I'm in exactly the same situation, first leaves appearing, plant is dying.

The seedlings start off a good colour, but very suddenly yellow, or lose their colour on the seed leaves. I've attached photos of plants which 2 days ago looked very green, although I think for 2 weeks of growth these plants are behind where they should be.

Things I've tried:

  • Using pots instead of propagator to give seedlings more soil depth.
  • Watering a tiny amount each day just enough to keep soil moist.
  • 3 different seed varieties at this point.

I've not had this issue in the past. Generally I'd start the seeds on the propagator and re-pot them to a large pot once the first set of seed leaves were somewhat established.

Anyone have any suggestions? I'm going to plant more straight into a large container this time and see if that helps.

Photos of my current run attached:

items.title: Frost/cold tolerance of Calendula officinalis as seedlings?
items.body:
One aspect of Calendula officinalis ("Pot Marigold") that attracted me to trying it was that it is reputed to take temperatures down to about 20°F (-7°C) without expiring in the fall, unlike Tagetes marigolds which pack up as soon as it frosts. In many years, that will get me a few weeks or even a month more flowering time in late fall.

Image source (my seed supplier for these, just a regular customer)

I'm now curious as to how much frost/cold seedlings (after appropriate hardening off) may take, knowing that some plants have very different frost tolerance as seedlings than as mature plants. If they can be planted out early, that's one less thing taking up the ever-shrinking space under the lights, as the cold intolerant seedlings grow larger.

items.title: Dead main trunk with new shoots at the bottom: Cut the trunk?
items.body:
Picture says more than a 1000 words, so let me start with it:

You can see the main trunk is completely dead. But at the very bottom, there are a few new shoots/branches which create a healthy looking canopy.

Can I cut the dead trunk and if so where?

What's the best way to make this tree continue to grow and preferably taller (instead of wider)?

items.title: How to shorten bamboo growing indoors?
items.body:
I’m growing some bamboo shoots indoors in a vase, but they have become too tall. They have a long stem with leaves at the top. I’m considering two ways to shorten them. One is to simply cut them short and hope new roots will grow. The other is to put them in very deep water (about 2ft deep) and wait for new roots to grow from the stems and then cut them short.

Which is the best way? Or is there another alternative?

Just thought of another possibility; could I graft a shortened stem back onto its own root stock?

Just don't know how bamboo will respond to any of these procedures.
